1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
47	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
48	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
49	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
50	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
51	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
52	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
53	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
54	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
55	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
56	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
57	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
58	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
59	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
60	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
61	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
62	
63	  The formal target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic} regards \(M\) as an
64	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
65	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
66	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}
67	  is free over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
68	\end{theorem}
69	
70	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
71	% exactly this signature):
72	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
73	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
74	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
75	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
76	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
77	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
78	
79	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
80	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
81	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
82	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
83	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
84	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
85	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
86	% there exists a finite filtration
87	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
88	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
89	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
90	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
91	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
92	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
93	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
94	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
95	\begin{proof}
96	
97	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
98	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
99	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
100	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
101	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
102	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
103	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
104	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
105	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
106	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
108	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
109	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
110	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
111	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
112	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
113	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
114	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
115	
116	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
117	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
118	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
119	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
120	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
121	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
122	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
123	  for exactly this residue.
124	
125	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction), as a \texttt{\textbackslash uses}-linked chain.}
126	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
127	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
128	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
129	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
130	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
131	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
132	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
133	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
134	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
135	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
136	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
137	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
138	\end{proof}
139	
140	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
141	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
142	
143	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
144	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
145	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
146	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each
147	\texttt{\textbackslash uses} only earlier blocks (or Mathlib anchors). Throughout,
148	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
149	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
150	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
151	
152	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
153	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
155	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
156	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
157	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
158	\end{lemma}
159	
160	\begin{proof}
161	
162	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction and is already closed in Lean
163	  axiom-clean. A finite module over a noetherian ring is finitely presented, so
164	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
165	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
166	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
167	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
168	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
169	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
170	  \(f := r\).
171	\end{proof}
172	
173	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
174	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
175	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
176	  \mathlibok
177	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
179	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
180	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
181	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r = \texttt{Localization.Away}~r\) (of the same finite
182	  rank).
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
186	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
188	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
189	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
191	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
192	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
193	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
194	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
195	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
196	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
197	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
198	  induction).}
199	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
200	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
201	  \(A_f\).
202	\end{lemma}
203	
204	\begin{proof}
205	
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
207	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
208	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
209	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
210	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
211	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
212	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
213	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
214	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
215	\end{proof}
216	
217	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
218	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
219	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
220	  \mathlibok
221	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
222	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
223	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
224	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
225	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
226	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
227	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
228	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
229	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
230	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
231	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
232	\end{lemma}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
235	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
236	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
237	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
238	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
239	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
240	  exact sequence
241	  \[
242	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
243	  \]
244	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular the localised map \(i_f\) is injective and the
245	  localised map \(q_f\) is surjective. Here \(A_f = \texttt{Localization.Away}~f\)
246	  and \(M_f = \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers}~f)\,M\), the localisation
247	  taken at the submonoid of powers of \(f\) after restricting scalars to \(A\).
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	
252	  Localisation of modules at a multiplicative set is an exact functor: it preserves
253	  injections, surjections, and exactness at the middle term. Applying it to the
254	  scalar-restricted maps \(i\) and \(q\) (regarded as \(A\)-linear via the tower
255	  \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence, exact in all three positions. In the
256	  formalisation this is the localisation-preserves-exactness API
257	  (\texttt{LocalizedModule.map\_exact}) applied to \(i\) and \(q\) regarded as
258	  \(A\)-linear maps via the scalar restriction along \(A \to B\).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
262	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
264	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
265	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
266	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
267	\end{lemma}
268	
269	\begin{proof}
270	
271	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
272	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
273	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
274	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
275	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
276	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
277	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
278	\end{proof}
279	
280	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
281	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
283	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
284	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
285	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
286	\end{lemma}
287	
288	\begin{proof}
289	
290	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
291	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
292	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
293	\end{proof}
294	
295	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
296	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
298	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
299	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
300	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
301	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
303	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
304	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
305	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
306	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
307	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
308	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
309	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
310	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
311	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
312	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
313	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
314	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
315	\end{lemma}
316	
317	\begin{proof}
318	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
319	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
320	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
322	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
323	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
324	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
325	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
326	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
327	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
328	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
329	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
330	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
331	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
332	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
333	  free over \(A_f\).
334	\end{proof}

336	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
337	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
338	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
339	  \mathlibok
340	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
341	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
342	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
343	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
344	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
345	\end{lemma}
346	
347	\begin{lemma}
348	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
349	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
351	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
352	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
353	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
354	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
355	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
356	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
357	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
358	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
359	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
360	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
361	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
362	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
363	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
364	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
365	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
366	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
367	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
368	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
369	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
370	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
371	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
372	  %         p ∈ Set.range
373	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
374	  % NOTE (iter-008 encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
375	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
376	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
377	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
378	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
379	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
380	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
381	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
382	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
383	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
384	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
385	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
386	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
387	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
388	\end{lemma}
389	
390	\begin{proof}
391	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
392	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
393	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
394	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
395	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
396	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
397	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
398	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
399	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
400	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
401	\end{proof}

403	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
404	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
406	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
407	  % NOTE (resolved iter-006): the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
408	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
409	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
410	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
411	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
412	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
413	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
414	  % The conclusion is:
415	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
416	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
417	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
418	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
419	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
420	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
421	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
422	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
423	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
424	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
425	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
426	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
427	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
428	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
429	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
430	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
431	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
432	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
433	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
434	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
435	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
436	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
437	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
438	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
439	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
440	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
441	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
442	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
443	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
444	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
445	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
446	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
447	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
448	  normalisation with denominators).}
449	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
450	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
451	  \(A_g = \texttt{Localization.Away}~g\) and
452	  \(B_g = \texttt{Localization.Away (algebraMap } A\,B\,g)\) for the localisations
453	  away from \(g\) and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element
454	  \(g \in A\) with \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
455	  \[
456	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
457	  \]
458	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] = \texttt{MvPolynomial (Fin } n)~A_g\) is the
459	  polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is
460	  module-finite over the image polynomial subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity
461	  of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic independence of the generators
462	  \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and the module-finiteness is taken over the algebra
463	  structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\). Equivalently, the classical statement
464	  ``\(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)'' is encoded by the single map
465	  \(\varphi\): it both names the polynomial subalgebra (as the domain
466	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)) and carries the algebra structure under which \(B_g\) is
467	  finite.
468	\end{lemma}
469	
470	\begin{proof}
471	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
472	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
473	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
474	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
475	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
476	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
477	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
478	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
479	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
480	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
481	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
482	
483	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
484	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
485	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
486	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
487	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
488	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
489	
490	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
491	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
492	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
493	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
494	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
495	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
496	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
497	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
498	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
499	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
500	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
501	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
502	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
503	
504	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
505	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
506	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
507	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
508	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
509	  image of \(\varphi\).
510	\end{proof}

512	\begin{lemma}
513	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
514	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
516	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
517	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
518	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
519	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
520	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
521	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
522	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
523	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
524	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
525	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
526	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
527	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
528	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
529	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
530	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
531	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
532	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
533	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
534	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
535	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
536	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
537	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
538	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
539	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
540	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
541	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
542	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
543	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
544	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
545	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
546	  \[
547	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
548	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
549	  \]
550	
551	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
552	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
553	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
554	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
555	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
556	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
557	\end{lemma}
558	
559	\begin{proof}
560	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
561	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
562	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
563	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
564	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
565	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
566	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
567	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
568	
569	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
570	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
571	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
572	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
573	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
574	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
575	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
576	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
577	
578	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
579	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
580	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
581	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
582	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
583	\end{proof}

595	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
596	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
597	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
598	  \mathlibok
599	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
600	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
601	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
602	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
603	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
604	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
605	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
606	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
607	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
608	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
609	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
610	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
611	\end{lemma}

613	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
614	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
615	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
616	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
617	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
618	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
619	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
620	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
621	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
622	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
623	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
624	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
625	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
626	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
627	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
628	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
629	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
630	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
631	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
632	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
633	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
634	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
635	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
636	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
637	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
638	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
639	\end{lemma}
640	
641	\begin{proof}
642	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
643	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
644	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
645	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
646	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
647	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
648	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
649	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
650	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
651	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
652	\end{proof}

654	\begin{lemma}[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
655	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
657	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
658	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
659	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
660	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
661	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
662	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
663	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
664	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
665	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
666	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
667	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
668	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
669	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
670	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
671	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
672	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
673	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
674	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
675	  %         IsUnit
676	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
677	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
678	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
679	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
680	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
681	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
682	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
683	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
684	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — viewed via \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\) as a univariate polynomial
685	  in \(X_0\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) — has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient (namely
686	  the image of \(g\)). Equivalently, \(e(F)\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over
687	  \(A_g\).
688	\end{lemma}
689	
690	\begin{proof}
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
692	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
693	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
694	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
695	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
696	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
697	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
698	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
699	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
700	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
701	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
702	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
703	\end{proof}

720	  % (lem:gf_clear_one_denominator / lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, after clearing
721	  % denominators) consume. It is stated over a GENERAL base ring R so it is not tied to

723	  % "leading coefficient is a unit" (monic up to a unit) is what the inversion step in
724	  % each caller produces; the strict-monic Mathlib anchor is reached inside the proof by
725	  % rescaling by the unit.
726	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
727	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
728	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
729	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
730	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
731	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
732	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
733	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
734	  %   The MvPolynomial (Fin n) R-module structure on the quotient is via the constant
735	  %   inclusion R[X_1,…,X_n] ↪ R[X_0,…,X_n] (X_j ↦ X_{j+1}, i.e. MvPolynomial.rename
736	  %   Fin.succ) followed by Ideal.Quotient.mk. The prover may encode this instance
737	  %   differently (e.g. through (finSuccEquiv R n).symm ∘ Polynomial.C); the math claim
738	  %   is unaffected.
739	  %     (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ)
740	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)
741	  %       (hp : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).leadingCoeff) :
742	  %       letI : Algebra (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
743	  %           (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p}) :=
744	  %         ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
745	  %           (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ :
746	  %             MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →+* MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)).toAlgebra
747	  %       Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
748	  %         (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})
749	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
750	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
751	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
752	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) (\(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\)) has a
753	  \emph{unit} leading coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
754	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
755	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
756	\end{lemma}
757	
758	\begin{proof}
759	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
760	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
761	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
762	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the \(R\)-algebra
763	  isomorphism \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\), which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and
764	  embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
765	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
766	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
767	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
768	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
769	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
770	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
771	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
772	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
773	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
774	\end{proof}

777	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
778	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}

815	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
816	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
817	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
818	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
819	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
820	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
821	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
822	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
823	\end{lemma}
824	
825	\begin{proof}
826	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
827	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
828	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
829	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
830	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
831	  is \(X_0\), the one eliminated by \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\); cf.\ the variable-convention
832	  note on \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
833	
834	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
835	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
836	
837	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
838	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
839	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
840	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
841	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
842	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
843	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
844	
845	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
846	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
847	  \(e(F)\) (whose \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\)-leading coefficient is a unit): the quotient
848	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
849	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
850	  \(A_g\).
851	
852	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
853	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
854	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
855	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
856	\end{proof}

889	  Nitsure \S4 induction, on which the whole finite-type case rests.

892	\begin{proof}
893	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
894	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
895	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
896	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
897	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
898	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE (iter-007): the induction must generalise the base domain A
899	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
900	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
901	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
902	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
903	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
904	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
905	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
906	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
907	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
908	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
909	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
910	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
911	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
912	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
913	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
914	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
915	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
916	  corresponding polynomial ring.
917	
918	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
919	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
920	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
921	
922	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
923	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
924	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
925	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
926	  \begin{enumerate}
927	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
928	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
929	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
930	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
931	      \(P_d\)-modules
932	      \[
933	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
934	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
935	      \]
936	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
937	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
938	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
939	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
940	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
941	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
942	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
943	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
944	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
945	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
946	      to \(A_g\) (see the structure note above).
947	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Writing \(h = a / g^k\) with
948	      \(a \in A\), the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is isomorphic to \(A_{g a}\) and
949	      \((T_g)_h\) to \(T_{g a}\); thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over \((A_g)_h\) transports
950	      to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the localisation-tower free-transport
951	      fact \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}. Set \(f := g a \ne 0\); then
952	      \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
953	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
954	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
955	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
956	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
957	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
958	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
959	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
960	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
961	      completes the induction.
962	  \end{enumerate}
963	\end{proof}

965	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
966	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
967	
968	The following two file-internal helpers in the \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace package
969	the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms (flatness and module-finiteness) that the
970	chain and the geometric globalization consume. Both are already proved axiom-clean.
971	
972	\begin{lemma}
973	
974	[Flat localization away, finite module]
975	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
976	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
977	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
978	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
979	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is
980	  \emph{flat} over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
981	\end{lemma}
982	\begin{proof}
983	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
984	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} the localized module is free over
985	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}; free modules are flat (\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}).
986	\end{proof}
987	
988	\begin{lemma}
989	
990	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
991	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
993	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
994	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra
995	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite A B]}), and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module
996	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite B M]}) with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the
997	  scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such
998	  that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is free over
999	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
1000	\end{lemma}
1001	\begin{proof}
1002	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1003	  The \(A\)-finiteness of \(M\) is \emph{derived}, not assumed: since \(B\) is
1004	  module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\), composition along
1005	  the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\) makes \(M\) module-finite over \(A\) (transitivity
1006	  of module-finiteness, \texttt{Module.Finite.trans}). With \(M\) now a finite
1007	  \(A\)-module, \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} produces the required \(f \ne 0\) with
1008	  \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} free over
1009	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}. The lemma is therefore strictly more general than a
1010	  repackaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: the finite \(A\)-algebra \(B\) and the
1011	  finite \(B\)-module \(M\) are the genuine inputs, and \(A\)-finiteness is a
1012	  consequence of the hypotheses rather than one of them.
1013	\end{proof}

1016	\begin{theorem}
1017	
1018	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1019	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1021	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1022	  % NOTE (re-signed iter-002): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1023	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1024	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any

1027	  % now the source of truth for the exact binders and the full flatness conclusion.

1030	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1031	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:

1033	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)

1036	  %

1038	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1039	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
1040	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1041	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1042	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1043	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1044	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1045	
1046	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1047	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1048	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1049	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1050	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1051	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1052	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1053	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1054	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1055	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1056	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1057	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1058	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1059	
1060	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
1061	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
1062	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
1063	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
1064	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
1065	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
1066	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
1067	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
1068	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
1069	\end{theorem}
1070	
1071	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1072	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1073	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1074	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1075	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1076	% by restricting to a non-empty
1077	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1078	\begin{proof}
1079	  
1080	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1081	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
1082	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
1083	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1084	
1085	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1086	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1087	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1088	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1089	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1090	
1091	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1092	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
1093	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
1094	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
1095	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
1096	  to the algebraic form.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1099	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1100	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1101	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1102	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
1103	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
1104	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
1105	
1106	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
1107	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
1108	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
1109	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
1110	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
1111	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
1112	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1113	\end{proof}

1116	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1117	
1118	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1119	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1120	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1121	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1122	generic-flatness argument consumes
1123	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1124	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1125	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1126	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1127	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1128	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1129	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1130	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1131	are introduced in
1132	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
1133	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
1134	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.

1019	  \mathlibok
1020	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1021	  Let \(R \to A \to B\) be a tower of algebras, \(M\) a \(B\)-module that is also an
1022	  \(A\)- and \(R\)-module compatibly, and \(N\) an \(R\)-module. Then there is a
1023	  \(B\)-linear equivalence, carrying \emph{no} flatness hypothesis,
1024	  \[
1025	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1026	    M \otimes_A (A \otimes_R N) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M \otimes_R N,
1027	  \]
1028	  with \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\bigl(m \otimes_A (a \otimes_R n)\bigr)
1029	  = (a \cdot m) \otimes_R n\) (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}) and inverse
1030	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}(m \otimes_R n) = m \otimes_A (1 \otimes_R n)\)
1031	  (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}). Instantiated at the affine--affine model
1032	  (\(M = R'\), \(B = R'\), \(N = M\)) this is the cancellation isomorphism
1033	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) used below, read in either
1034	  orientation.

1189	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1190	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1191	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1193	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1194	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1195	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1196	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1197	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1198	\end{lemma}
1199	\begin{proof}
1200	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1201	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1202	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1203	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1204	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1205	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1206	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1207	\end{proof}

1209	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1210	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1211	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1213	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1214	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1215	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1216	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1217	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1221	  \cref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1222	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1223	\end{proof}

1228	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1229	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1230	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1231	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1232	  % NOTE (iter-003): tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1233	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1234	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1235	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1236	  \uses{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib, lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
1237	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1238	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1239	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1240	  % L933--938).
1241	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1242	  % equality
1243	  % $$
1244	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1245	  % $$
1246	  % as $R'$-modules."
1247	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1248	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1249	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1250	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1251	  \[
1252	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1253	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1254	  \]
1255	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1256	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1257	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1258	  hypothesis.
1259	\end{lemma}
1260	
1261	\begin{proof}
1262	  \uses{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib, lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
1263	  The underlying additive bijection is supplied by the standalone, geometry-free
1264	  \(R'\)-linear equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}; the work
1265	  of this proof is to repackage that equivalence as a morphism of \(R'\)-module objects.
1266	  Two points of bookkeeping make the repackaging non-trivial, and pinning them down
1267	  \emph{is} the content of the proof. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1268	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1269	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1270	
1271	  \emph{The two \(A\)-actions are distinct types, so an identity bridge is mandatory.}
1272	  The source of the standalone equivalence, \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), is the
1273	  relative tensor product over the \emph{canonical} \(A\)-action on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1274	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the
1275	  carrier object produced by the dictionaries is
1276	  \((\operatorname{extendScalars}\iota)\,M\) with \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the
1277	  left inclusion, whose relative tensor product is taken over the
1278	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\iota\) \(A\)-action. These two \(A\)-module structures on
1279	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) coincide on elements, but the tensor product depends on the
1280	  \(A\)-module structure in its type, so the two tensor products are \emph{not equal}
1281	  even though their underlying abelian groups are the same. One therefore inserts the identity \(A\)-linear bridge
1282	  \[
1283	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M
1284	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1285	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M ,
1286	  \]
1287	  which re-reads the same generators against the carrier's \(A\)-action. This bridge is
1288	  the mathematically essential glue between the standalone helper and the object; it
1289	  cannot be elided.
1290	
1291	  \emph{The residue is the \(R'\)-linearity of the assembled additive map, checked on
1292	  generators.} After the bridge, the only remaining obligation is that the assembled
1293	  additive equivalence respects the \(R'\)-module structures. The \(R'\)-action on the
1294	  carrier is restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1295	  \(r' \mapsto 1 \otimes r'\), so \(r' \cdot y = (1 \otimes r') \cdot y\). On a
1296	  generator the extension-of-scalars action then computes
1297	  \[
1298	    (1 \otimes r') \cdot \bigl((a \otimes s) \otimes m\bigr)
1299	    = \bigl((1 \otimes r')(a \otimes s)\bigr) \otimes m
1300	    = \bigl(a \otimes (r' s)\bigr) \otimes m ,
1301	  \]
1302	  using \((1 \otimes r')(a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\) in \(A \otimes_R R'\). The
1303	  helper's underlying map sends \((a \otimes s) \otimes m \mapsto s \otimes (a \cdot m)\)
1304	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}), so it carries
1305	  \(\bigl(a \otimes (r' s)\bigr) \otimes m\) to
1306	  \((r' s) \otimes (a \cdot m) = r' \cdot \bigl(s \otimes (a \cdot m)\bigr)\), which is
1307	  \(r'\) times the image of the original generator. Thus the additive map commutes with
1308	  the \(R'\)-action on generators, hence everywhere by bilinearity; together with the
1309	  bridge \(eT\) this promotes the standalone equivalence to the desired bundled
1310	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism. Being a composite of linear equivalences the result is an
1311	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents carries a flatness hypothesis.
1312	\end{proof}

1342	\begin{proof}
1343	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
1344	  The unit of an extension-of-scalars adjunction sends \(m\) to the elementary tensor
1345	  \(1 \otimes m\). Under the pullback dictionary
1346	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, naturality at the unit identifies
1347	  \((g')^*\widetilde M\) on global sections with the extension of scalars
1348	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) along \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\) and carries the unit
1349	  element \(1 \otimes m\) to \((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\). Composing with the codomain
1350	  read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}
1351	  leaves the value \((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) on the generator \(m\).
1352	\end{proof}

1354	\begin{lemma}
1355	[Pseudofunctor reindexing of the pushforward target]
1356	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}
1357	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}
1358	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1359	  In the affine--affine model, applying \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) to the
1360	  unit assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of
1361	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value} leaves it unchanged on elements
1362	  (restriction of scalars is the identity on underlying sets), and the pseudofunctor
1363	  identities \((g'f)_* = (f'g)_* = g_*\,f'_*\) (forced by the commutativity
1364	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square) reindex the target as the \(R'\)-module
1365	  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) of the codomain read
1366	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1367	\end{lemma}
1368	
1369	\begin{proof}
1370	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1371	  Restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) does not move elements, so \(f_*\) carries
1372	  the assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) to itself on underlying sets.
1373	  The coherence of the pushforward pseudofunctor under the equality
1374	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) — composition of pushforwards together with congruence
1375	  under equal morphisms — rewrites \((g'f)_*\widetilde M\) as
1376	  \(g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\), whose inner object
1377	  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) is read by
1378	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} as \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1379	  This reindexing is exactly the in-file pushforward-composition and
1380	  pushforward-congruence naturality applied to the codomain dictionary; no new content
1381	  beyond those category-level identities is needed.
1382	\end{proof}

1384	\begin{lemma}
1385	[Transpose-on-elements formula for the base-change adjunction]
1386	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
1387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
1388	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1389	  % NOTE: the (g^* ⊣ g_*) adjunction here is the affine base-change /
1390	  % extension-restriction adjunction along ψ : R → R'; the formula below is the
1391	  % standard transpose-on-elements identity for an extension-of-scalars adjunction,
1392	  % so it stands on its proof sketch with no external reference.
1393	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\). For an \(R\)-linear map
1394	  \(u : \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M \to \operatorname{restr}_\psi N\), the
1395	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose \(\widehat u\) is the unique \(R'\)-linear map
1396	  \(\widehat u : R' \otimes_R M \to N\) determined on generators by
1397	  \(\widehat u(r' \otimes m) = r' \cdot u(m)\). Applied to the unit assignment
1398	  \(u : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of
1399	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}, with the source read as
1400	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}, the transpose is
1401	  \[
1402	    r' \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
1403	    \;=\; (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m ,
1404	  \]
1405	  the \(R'\)-action scaling the \(R'\)-tensor factor of \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1406	\end{lemma}
1407	
1408	\begin{proof}
1409	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1410	  The transpose under an extension-of-scalars adjunction
1411	  \(g^* = R' \otimes_R (-)\) is the unique \(R'\)-linear extension of \(u\) along the
1412	  unit \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m\), so it is determined on generators by
1413	  \(\widehat u(r' \otimes m) = r' \cdot u(m)\). Substituting
1414	  \(u(m) = (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) and using that the \(R'\)-action on
1415	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) scales the \(R'\)-tensor factor
1416	  (\(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\)) yields the stated
1417	  value. The source identification \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the domain read
1418	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}.
1419	\end{proof}

1435	  % $$

1438	  % as $R'$-modules."
