1440	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1441	  With the domain and codomain pinned by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}
1442	  and Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}, the conjugated section-level
1443	  base-change map
1444	  \[
1445	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} :
1446	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
1447	  \]
1448	  sends \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\); equivalently it equals the
1449	  \emph{inverse} of the regrouping isomorphism
1450	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}, i.e.\
1451	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}

1455	\begin{proof}

1457	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv,
1458	        lem:base_change_mate_unit_value, lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex,
1459	        lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
1460	  By Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \(\alpha\) is the
1461	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of \(f_*(\eta')\), with \(\eta'\) the
1462	  \(\bigl((g')^*, (g')_*\bigr)\)-unit. The computation is the thin assembly of three
1463	  isolated steps, each reading the relevant functor on global modules through the
1464	  dictionaries of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} and
1465	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1466	  \begin{itemize}
1467	    \item[(A)] \emph{The unit value.} By
1468	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}, the \(\bigl((g')^*,(g')_*\bigr)\)-unit
1469	      \(\eta'\), read through \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), is the base-change unit
1470	      \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1471	    \item[(C)] \emph{Pushforward and reindex.} By
1472	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}, applying
1473	      \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) leaves this assignment unchanged on
1474	      elements and reindexes the target, via the pseudofunctor identities, as the
1475	      codomain read \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1476	    \item[(B)] \emph{Transpose under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\).} By
1477	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, the transpose of the assignment
1478	      \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) is the \(R'\)-linear map
1479	      \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)

1481	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}.
1482	  \end{itemize}

1694	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
1695	  \]
1696	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
1697	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
1698	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
1699	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
1700	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
1701	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
1702	  fails.
1703	\end{lemma}

1705	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
1706	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
1707	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
1708	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
1709	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
1710	% for some $A$-module $M$.
1711	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
1712	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
1713	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
1714	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
1715	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
1716	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1717	% equality
1718	% $$
1719	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1720	% $$
1721	% as $R'$-modules."
1722	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
1723	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
1724	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
1725	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
1726	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --
1727	% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
1728	% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.
1729	\begin{proof}
1730	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
1731	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
1732	  global sections}.
1733	
1734	  \medskip
1735	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
1736	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
1737	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
1738	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
1739	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
1740	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
1741	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
1742	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
1743	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
1744	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
1745	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
1746	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
1747	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
1748	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
1749	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
1750	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
1751	
1752	  \medskip
1753	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
1754	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
1755	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
1756	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
1757	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
1758	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
1759	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
1760	  \[
1761	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
1762	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
1763	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
1764	  \]
1765	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
1766	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
1767	  of modules.
1768	
1769	  \medskip
1770	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
1771	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
1772	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
1773	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
1774	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
1775	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
1776	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
1777	  \[
1778	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1779	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
1780	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
1781	  \]
1782	  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
1783	  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). This is the entire
1784	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
1785	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
1786	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
1787	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
1788	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
1789	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
1790	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
1791	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
1792	\end{proof}

1794	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
1795	
1796	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
1797	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
1798	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

1800	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
1801	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1802	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
1803	  \mathlibok
1804	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1805	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
1806	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
1807	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
1808	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
1809	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
1810	  \[
1811	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
1812	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1813	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
1814	  \]
1815	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
1816	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
1817	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
1818	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
1819	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
1820	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
1821	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
1822	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
1823	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
1824	\end{lemma}

1826	\begin{theorem}\leanok
1827	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
1828	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
1829	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
1830	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
1831	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1832	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
1833	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
1834	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
1835	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
1836	  % $$
1837	  % \xymatrix{
1838	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
1839	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
1840	  % }
1841	  % $$
1842	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1843	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
1844	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
1845	  % For any $i \geq 0$
1846	  % \begin{enumerate}
1847	  % \item the base change map of
1848	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
1849	  % is an isomorphism
1850	  % $$
1851	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
1852	  % $$
1853	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
1854	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
1855	  % \end{enumerate}"
1856	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
1857	  \(i = 0\) case.}
1858	  Consider the cartesian square
1859	  \[
1860	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1861	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
1862	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
1863	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
1864	    \end{array}
1865	  \]
1866	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
1867	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
1868	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
1869	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1870	  \[
1871	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1872	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
1873	  \]
1874	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
1875	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
1876	  \[
1877	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1878	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1879	  \]
1880	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
1881	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
1882	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
1883	
1884	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
1885	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
1886	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
1887	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
1888	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
1889	  an isomorphism.
1890	\end{theorem}

1907	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
1908	%
1909	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
1910	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
1911	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1912	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
1913	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
1914	% We are trying to show that the map
1915	% $$
1916	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1917	% $$
1918	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
1919	% is an isomorphism.

1923	% $$

1925	% $$
1926	% see
1927	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
1928	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
1929	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
1930	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
1931	% $$
1932	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1933	% $$
1934	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
1935	% $$
1936	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
1937	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1938	% $$
1939	% as follows from
1940	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
1941	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology.
1942	%
1943	% In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open covering
1944	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
1945	% {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
1946	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
1947	% The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
1948	% can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
1949	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
1950	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
1951	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
1952	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
1953	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
1954	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
1955	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
1956	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
1957	\begin{proof}
1958	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1959	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
1960	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
1961	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
1962	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
1963	  sequence.
1964	
1965	  \medskip
1966	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
1967	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
1968	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
1969	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1970	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
1971	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
1972	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1973	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
1974	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
1975	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
1976	  \[
1977	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1978	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1979	  \]
1980	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
1981	
1982	  \medskip
1983	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
1984	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
1985	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
1986	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
1987	  \[
1988	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1989	    \;=\;
1990	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
1991	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
1992	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
1993	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
1994	    \Bigr),
1995	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
1996	  \]
1997	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
1998	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
1999	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2000	  higher cohomology.
2001	
2002	  \medskip
2003	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2004	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2005	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2006	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2007	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2008	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2009	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2010	  \[
2011	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2012	    \qquad
2013	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2014	  \]
2015	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2016	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2017	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2018	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2019	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, i.e.\ Mathlib's
2020	  \texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}):
2021	  \[
2022	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2023	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2024	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2025	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2026	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2027	  \]
2028	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2029	
2030	  \medskip
2031	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2032	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2033	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2034	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2035	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2036	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2037	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2038	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2039	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2040	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2041	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2042	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2043	  \[
2044	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2045	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2046	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2047	  \]
2048	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2049	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2050	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2051	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2052	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2053	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2054	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE (iter-007): blocked on absent tensor/monoidal structure for SheafOfModules.
160	  % The degree-m component is Gamma(X_s, L_s^{otimes m}); Mathlib at the pinned commit has no
161	  % MonoidalCategoryStruct on X.Modules (synthesis fails) and no SheafOfModules/PresheafOfModules
162	  % tensor lemmas, so L^{otimes m} cannot even be named. This is a deep infrastructure prerequisite
163	  % (tensor product of sheaves of modules + lax-monoidal global sections), to be blueprinted as a
164	  % separate multi-lemma sub-build. See task_results .../QuotScheme.md.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}
199	
200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}
306	
307	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
308	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
310	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
311	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
312	  % Polynomials" (read from
313	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
315	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
316	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
317	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
318	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
319	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
320	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
322	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
323	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
324	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
325	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
326	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
327	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
328	  such that for all \(n > N\)
329	  \[
330	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
331	  \]
332	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
333	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
334	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
335	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
336	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
337	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
338	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
339	
340	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
341	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
342	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
344	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
345	  rational series from the module.
346	\end{lemma}
347	
348	\begin{proof}
349	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
350	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
351	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
352	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
353	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
354	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
355	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
356	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
357	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
358	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
359	  graded modules
360	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
361	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
362	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
363	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
364	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
365	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
366	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
367	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
368	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
369	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
370	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
374	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
376	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
377	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
378	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
379	  % Polynomials" (read from
380	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
381	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
382	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
383	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
384	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
385	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
386	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
387	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
388	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
389	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
390	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
391	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
392	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
393	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
394	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
395	  \[
396	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
397	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
398	  \]
399	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
400	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
401	\end{theorem}
402	
403	\begin{proof}
404	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
405	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
406	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
407	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
408	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
409	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
410	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
411	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
412	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
413	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
414	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
415	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
416	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
417	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
418	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
419	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
420	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
421	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
422	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
426	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
427	
428	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
429	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
430	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
431	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
432	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
433	
434	\begin{definition}
435	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
436	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
437	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
438	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
439	  % NOTE: basic-open coherence depends on lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen
440	  % (section restriction as a localization for quasi-coherent sheaves on a general scheme),
441	  % which is not yet formally closed.
442	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
443	  % Polynomials" (read from
444	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
445	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
446	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
447	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
448	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
449	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
450	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
451	  project-built primitive.}
452	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
453	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
454	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
455	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
456	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
457	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
458	  \(U \subseteq X\),
459	  \[
460	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
461	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
462	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
463	  \]
464	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
465	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
466	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
467	
468	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
469	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
470	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
471	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
472	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
473	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
474	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
475	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
476	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
477	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
478	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
479	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
480	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
481	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
482	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
483	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
484	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
485	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
486	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
487	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
488	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
489	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
490	
491	  \emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
492	  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
493	\end{definition}
494	
495	\begin{lemma}\leanok
496	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
497	  localization]
498	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
499	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
500	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
501	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
502	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
503	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
504	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
505	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
506	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
507	  \[
508	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
509	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
510	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
511	  \]
512	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
513	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
514	
515	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
516	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
517	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
518	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
519	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
520	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
521	\end{lemma}
522	
523	\begin{proof}
524	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
525	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
526	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
527	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
528	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
529	
530	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
531	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
532	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
533	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
534	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
535	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
536	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
537	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
538	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
539	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
540	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
541	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
542	\end{proof}
543	
544	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
545	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
547	  \mathlibok
548	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
549	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
550	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
551	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
552	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
553	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
554	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
555	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
556	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
557	\end{lemma}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
560	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
562	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
563	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
564	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
565	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
566	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
567	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
568	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
569	  %  associated to $M$.
570	  %  [...]
571	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
572	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
573	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
574	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
575	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on \(X\)
576	  that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type} (over a locally noetherian
577	  base, matching the coherence encoding under which \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) is a
578	  finite \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module on each affine open). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
579	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
580	  \begin{enumerate}
581	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
582	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
583	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
584	    \item the section-restriction map
585	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
586	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
587	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
588	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
589	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
590	  \end{enumerate}
591	\end{lemma}
592	
593	\begin{proof}
594	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
595	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
596	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
597	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
598	
599	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
600	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf of finite type is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
601	  finite \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
602	  \(N\). The Spec-local computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
603	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
604	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
605	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
606	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
607	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
608	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
609	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
610	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
611	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
612	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
613	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
614	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
615	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
616	\end{proof}
617	
618	\begin{definition}
619	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
620	  \label{def:schematic_support}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
622	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
623	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
624	  % Polynomials" (read from
625	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
626	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
627	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
628	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
629	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
630	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
631	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
632	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
633	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
634	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
635	  \[
636	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
637	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
638	  \]
639	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
640	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
641	  \(X\).
642	\end{definition}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
645	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
647	  \mathlibok
648	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
649	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
650	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
651	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
652	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
653	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
654	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
655	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
656	\end{lemma}
657	
658	\begin{definition}
659	[Proper support over the base]
660	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
661	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
662	  \uses{def:schematic_support, lem:isProper_mathlib}
663	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
664	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
665	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
666	  % + L418-L419).
667	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
668	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
669	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
670	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
671	  %  [...]
672	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
673	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
674	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
675	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
676	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
677	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
678	  \[
679	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
680	  \]
681	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
682	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
683	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
684	  functor's pullback action requires.
685	\end{definition}
686	
687	\begin{definition}
688	
689	\leanok
690	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
691	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
692	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
693	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
694	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
695	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
696	  % + L584-L585).
697	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
698	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
699	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
700	  %  [...]
701	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
702	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
703	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
704	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
705	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
706	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
707	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for
708	  each \(i\).
709	
710	  \emph{Note.} This predicate is project-built; it is not supplied by Mathlib.
711	\end{definition}

713	\section{The Quot functor}
714	\label{sec:quot_functor}
715	
716	\begin{definition}\leanok
717	[Quot functor]
718	  \label{def:quot_functor}
719	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
720	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
721	
722	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
723	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
724	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
725	  % + L481-L498).
726	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
727	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
728	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
729	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
730	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
731	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
732	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
733	  %
734	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
735	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
736	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
737	  %
738	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
739	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
740	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
741	  %
742	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
743	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
744	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
745	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
746	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
747	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
748	  %  well-defined, which gives
749	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
750	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
751	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
752	  %  [...]
753	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
754	  %  as a co-product
755	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
756	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
757	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
758	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
759	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
760	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
761	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
762	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
763	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
764	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
765	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
766	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
767	  \[
768	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
769	  \]
770	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
771	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
772	  \begin{enumerate}
773	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
774	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
775	      which is flat over \(T\);
776	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
777	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
778	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
779	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
780	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
781	      \(\Phi\).
782	  \end{enumerate}
783	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
784	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
785	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
786	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
787	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
788	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
789	\end{definition}
790	
791	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
792	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}

794	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
795	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
796	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
797	linear algebra), we package the construction here.

837	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the

927	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
928	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding

934	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
935	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
936	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
937	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
938	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
939	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
940	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
941	\begin{proof}
942	  
943	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
944	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
945	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
946	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
947	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
948	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
949	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
950	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
951	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
952	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
953	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
954	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
955	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
956	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
957	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
958	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
959	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
960	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
961	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
962	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
963	
964	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
965	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
966	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
967	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
968	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
969	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
970	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
971	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
972	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
973	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
974	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
975	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
976	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
977	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
978	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
979	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
980	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
981	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
982	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
983	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
984	\end{proof}

23	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
24	\label{sec:gr_setup}
25	
26	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
27	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
28	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
29	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
30	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
31	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
32	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
33	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
34	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
35	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
36	
37	\begin{definition}
38	
39	\leanok
40	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
41	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}

43	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
44	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
45	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
48	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
49	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
50	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
51	  %  $I$.
52	  %
53	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
54	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
55	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
56	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
57	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
58	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically

60	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
61	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
62	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
63	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
64	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
65	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
66	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
67	  set
68	  \[
69	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
70	  \]
71	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
72	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
73	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
74	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
75	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
76	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
