92	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
93	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
94	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
95	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
96	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
97	  %  on $U^I_J$.
98	  %
99	  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
100	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
101	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
102	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
103	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
104	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
105	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
106	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
107	  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
108	  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
109	  minor of \(X^I\), and let
110	  \[
111	    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
112	  \]
113	  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
114	  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
115	  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
116	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
117	  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
118	  homomorphism
119	  \[
120	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
121	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
122	    \qquad
123	    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
124	  \]
125	  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
126	  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
127	  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
128	  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
129	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
130	  \[
131	    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
132	  \]
133	  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
134	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
135	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
136	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
137	  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\begin{lemma}
141	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
142	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
144	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
145	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
146	  % affine patches" (read from
147	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
148	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
149	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
150	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
151	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
152	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
153	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
154	  %  is satisfied."
155	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
156	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
157	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
158	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
159	  \[
160	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
161	  \]
162	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
163	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
164	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
165	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
166	  glue the charts.
167	\end{lemma}
168	
169	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
170	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
171	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
172	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
173	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
174	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
175	% L838-L848.)
176	\begin{proof}
177	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
178	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
179	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
180	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
181	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
182	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
183	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
184	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
185	
186	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
187	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
188	  \[
189	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
190	  \]
191	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
192	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
193	  \[
194	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
195	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
196	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
197	  \]
198	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
199	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
200	  \[
201	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
202	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
203	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
204	  \]
205	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
206	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
207	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
208	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
209	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
210	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
211	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
212	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
213	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
214	\end{proof}
215	
216	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
217	\label{sec:gr_glued}
218	
219	\begin{definition}
220	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
221	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
222	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
223	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
224	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
225	  % affine patches" (read from
226	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
227	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
228	  % "Therefore the schemes
229	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
230	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
231	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
232	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
233	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
234	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
235	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
236	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
237	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
238	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
239	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
240	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
241	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
242	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
243	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
244	  \[
245	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
246	  \]
247	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
248	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
249	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
250	
251	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
252	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
253	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
254	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
255	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
256	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
257	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
258	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
259	  follows.
260	\end{definition}
261	
262	\section{Separatedness}
263	\label{sec:gr_separated}
264	
265	\begin{lemma}
266	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
267	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
268	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
269	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
270	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
271	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
273	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
274	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
275	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
276	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
277	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
278	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
279	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
280	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
281	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
282	\end{lemma}
283	
284	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
285	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
286	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
287	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
288	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
291	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
292	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
293	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
294	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
295	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
296	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
297	  homomorphism is surjective.
298	
299	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
300	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
301	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
302	  \[
303	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
304	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
305	    \qquad
306	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
307	  \]
308	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
309	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
310	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
311	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
312	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
313	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
314	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
315	  and in particular
316	  \[
317	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
318	  \]
319	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
320	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
321	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
322	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
323	  \[
324	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
325	  \]
326	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
327	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
328	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
329	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
330	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
331	\end{proof}
332	
333	\section{Properness}
334	\label{sec:gr_proper}
335	
336	\begin{lemma}
337	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
338	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
340	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
341	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
344	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
345	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
346	  %  Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
347	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
348	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
349	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
350	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
351	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
352	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
353	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
354	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
355	  %
356	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
357	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
358	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
359	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
360	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
361	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
362	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
363	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
364	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
365	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
366	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
367	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
368	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
369	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
371	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
372	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
373	\end{lemma}
374	
375	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
376	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
377	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
378	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
379	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
380	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
381	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
382	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
383	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
384	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
385	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
386	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
387	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
388	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
389	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
390	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
391	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
392	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
393	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
394	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
395	\begin{proof}
396	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
397	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
398	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
399	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
400	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
401	  \[
402	    \begin{array}{ccc}
403	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
404	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
405	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
406	    \end{array}
407	  \]
408	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
409	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
410	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
411	
412	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
413	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
414	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
415	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
416	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
417	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
418	  \(K\). Define
419	  \[
420	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
421	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
422	  \]
423	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
424	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
425	  landing in \(U^J\).
426	
427	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
428	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
429	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
430	  \[
431	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
432	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
433	    \qquad
434	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
435	  \]
436	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
437	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
438	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
439	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
440	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
441	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
442	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
443	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
444	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
445	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
446	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
447	
448	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
449	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
450	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
451	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
452	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
453	  proper.
454	\end{proof}
455	
456	\section{Out of scope}
457	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
458	
459	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
460	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
461	and properness -- the GR-cells and GR-glue sub-phases. The following are
462	deliberately deferred to later sub-phases and are not treated here:
463	\begin{itemize}
464	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
465	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
466	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding (GR-quot).
467	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
468	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
469	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
470	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\) (GR-repr); this is blueprinted as
471	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
472	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
473	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
474	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
475	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
476	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
477	    that base change.
478	\end{itemize}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`

76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}

179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

225	  %  \nonumber

228	  %  The functor \(F\) is representable by a scheme."
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
230	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
232	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
233	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
234	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
235	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$

243	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).

249	% the subfunctors F_i associated to an affine cover, and identifying each F_i

254	  
255	  \leanok
256	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
257	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
258	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
259	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
260	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +
261	  % ``relativeGluingData.toBase_preimage_eq_opensRange_ι`` +
262	  % ``isAffineOpen_opensRange`` — a real proof against the gluing infrastructure,
263	  % NOT against a trivial identity placeholder. The Lean type remains the
264	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
265	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).

282	\section{Affine base}
283	\label{sec:relspec_affine}

285	\begin{theorem}\leanok
286	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
287	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
289	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
290	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
291	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
292	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
293	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).

295	  % is available.

299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
307	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]
311	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
312	  spectrum of the global sections.
313	\end{theorem}

345	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on

351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
352	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
357	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
359	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
360	  \(T \to \Spec A\) by the affine universal property
361	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
362	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
363	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
364	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
384	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.
389	
390	\section{Out of scope}
391	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
392	
393	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core
394	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
395	are explicitly out of scope here:
396	\begin{itemize}
397	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
398	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
399	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
400	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
401	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
402	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
403	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)
404	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
405	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
406	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
407	\end{itemize}
